what: 2 - Compute linear model on Standard Curves
title: abs-to-conc
text: Choose model wisely cover later script implement polynomial model rather linear model. dosage mineral N pools, model can necessary, example, concentrations (absorbance values) high. best red flag inappropriateness linear model fit curve seem suit plotting experimental points. new pipelines, always check standard curves graphically ensure good fit. See later, section Section 6 lm_std_curve() computes per-curve linear regression 2 columns input data lm(abs_corrected ~ 0 + std_conc, data = curve) linear model constraints curve go origin. lm_std_curve() returns table containing one row per standard curve, series information characterizing performance linear model curve (see also ?lm_std_curve(). lm_std_curve() defines curve based groups (dplyr::group_by()) input data (std_corrected example ). Additionally columns abs_corrected std_conc (numeric), function also requires column unique_curve_id. function suspicious_lm() extracts lm_table` produced plates linear model optimal, .e., either p-value model 0.05, residuals normally distributed, heteroscedasticity residuals. lm_table_suspicious can serve identification outliers visual aid (useful larger data sets, plot_list_lm() creates list plots curve given argument (: suspicious lm’s). Calling individual plots can help spotting possible outlier wells, can removed similar steps shown .  numerous suspicious curves, can take advantage package patchwork` display multiple plots (example hereunder whole lm_table)  point, may want remove obvious outlier wells. Follow steps shown vignettes prerequisites remove_wells(). Let’s say want remove well E12 plate NO3_1F1 dataset Nmin: now , longer use std_corrected, std_corrected_wash1. recommend always running one round quality check cleaned datasets approving regression equations. monstrously wrong wells removed single curves, case several curves pipetted per 96-well plate, still need perform per-dilution average absorbance. Indeed, 2 events pipetting dilution, rather 2 successive dilutions. WARNING next step computes per plate per row means standard curves. wells swapped plates, may cause problems. Make sure pipetting issue, correct raw data solve code std_dilution_average() creates artificial “column 13”. can now rerun linear model cleaned (required) per-dilution averaged, repeating steps : computation linear model, identification suspicious curves plotting Good news, suspicious linear models anymore. , one round QC described can still helpful (plot_list_lm()). Let’s store last correction clean variable name reduce possible confusion, let’s compute plots big list, storage purposes. export one output data single list readr::write_rds()`. TODO –> make function? Remove ? First, let’s look distribution p-values std curve regressions  , R_squared (adjusted?)  Now plot curves plot  Now, finally, decide happy standard curves, can move apply equations data
code: (lm_table_raw <- lm_std_curve(std_corrected |> dplyr::group_by(plate_id, column))) #> # A tibble: 10 × 12 #>    dataset plate_id unique_curve_id std_sp  slope r_squared adj_r_squared #>    <chr>   <chr>    <chr>           <chr>   <dbl>     <dbl>         <dbl> #>  1 Nmin    NO3_1F1  NO3_1F1_col1    NO3    0.0189     0.999         0.999 #>  2 Nmin    NO3_1F1  NO3_1F1_col12   NO3    0.0179     0.999         0.999 #>  3 Nmin    NO3_1F2  NO3_1F2_col1    NO3    0.0178     0.999         0.999 #>  4 Nmin    NO3_1F2  NO3_1F2_col12   NO3    0.0190     0.999         0.999 #>  5 Nmin    NO3_1F3  NO3_1F3_col1    NO3    0.0187     0.999         0.999 #>  6 Nmin    NO3_1F3  NO3_1F3_col12   NO3    0.0185     0.999         0.999 #>  7 Nmin    NO3_1F4  NO3_1F4_col1    NO3    0.0178     0.999         0.999 #>  8 Nmin    NO3_1F4  NO3_1F4_col12   NO3    0.0188     0.999         0.999 #>  9 Nmin    NO3_1F5  NO3_1F5_col1    NO3    0.0193     0.999         0.999 #> 10 Nmin    NO3_1F5  NO3_1F5_col12   NO3    0.0185     0.999         0.998 #> # ℹ 5 more variables: lm_p <dbl>, normality_lm_residuals <chr>, #> #   shapiro_p <dbl>, homoscedasticity_lm_residuals <chr>, breusch_pagan_p <dbl> # extract all plates where "something" is not perfect  (lm_table_suspicious <- lm_table_raw |> suspicious_lm()) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>   dataset plate_id unique_curve_id std_sp  slope r_squared adj_r_squared #>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>           <chr>   <dbl>     <dbl>         <dbl> #> 1 Nmin    NO3_1F1  NO3_1F1_col12   NO3    0.0179     0.999         0.999 #> # ℹ 5 more variables: lm_p <dbl>, normality_lm_residuals <chr>, #> #   shapiro_p <dbl>, homoscedasticity_lm_residuals <chr>, breusch_pagan_p <dbl> suspicious_lm_plotlist <- plot_list_lm(   lm_data = lm_table_suspicious,   std_data = std_corrected)  # check one plot out suspicious_lm_plotlist[[1]] full_plotlist <- plot_list_lm(   lm_data = lm_table_raw,   std_data = std_corrected)   patchwork::wrap_plots(full_plotlist, axis_titles = "collect_y") +      patchwork::plot_annotation(title = "Plots of suspicious Standard curves") to_remove <- tibble::tibble(   dataset = "Nmin",   plate_id = "NO3_1F1",   well_id = "E12" )  std_corrected_wash1 <- std_corrected |> remove_wells(to_remove) std_dilution_avg <- std_corrected_wash1 |> std_dilution_average() (lm_std_mean <- lm_std_curve(std_dilution_avg |> dplyr::rename(abs_corrected = abs_mean))) #> # A tibble: 5 × 12 #>   dataset plate_id unique_curve_id std_sp  slope r_squared adj_r_squared #>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>           <chr>   <dbl>     <dbl>         <dbl> #> 1 Nmin    NO3_1F1  NO3_1F1_col13   NO3    0.0184     0.999         0.999 #> 2 Nmin    NO3_1F2  NO3_1F2_col13   NO3    0.0184     0.999         0.999 #> 3 Nmin    NO3_1F3  NO3_1F3_col13   NO3    0.0186     0.999         0.999 #> 4 Nmin    NO3_1F4  NO3_1F4_col13   NO3    0.0183     0.999         0.999 #> 5 Nmin    NO3_1F5  NO3_1F5_col13   NO3    0.0189     0.999         0.999 #> # ℹ 5 more variables: lm_p <dbl>, normality_lm_residuals <chr>, #> #   shapiro_p <dbl>, homoscedasticity_lm_residuals <chr>, breusch_pagan_p <dbl> (lm_suspicious_mean <- lm_std_mean |> suspicious_lm()) #> # A tibble: 0 × 12 #> # ℹ 12 variables: dataset <chr>, plate_id <chr>, unique_curve_id <chr>, #> #   std_sp <chr>, slope <dbl>, r_squared <dbl>, adj_r_squared <dbl>, #> #   lm_p <dbl>, normality_lm_residuals <chr>, shapiro_p <dbl>, #> #   homoscedasticity_lm_residuals <chr>, breusch_pagan_p <dbl> std_data_clean <- std_dilution_avg lm_table_clean <- lm_std_mean lm_plots_clean <- plot_list_lm(   lm_table_clean, std_data_clean |> dplyr::rename(abs_corrected = abs_mean))  lm_output <- list(   "std_data_clean" = std_data_clean,   "lm_table_clean" = lm_table_clean,   "lm_plots_clean" = lm_plots_clean,   "sample_corrected" = sample_corrected )  # just an example of how to save this in a file for downstream steps #lm_output |> write_rds("output/data/lm_output.rds") p_threshold <- 0.05  lm_output$lm_table_clean |>    tidyr::separate_wider_delim(     cols = unique_curve_id, delim = "_",      names = c("n_sp", "rest"), too_many = "merge") |>   ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = -log(lm_p))) +    ggplot2::theme_minimal() +   # geom_histogram() +   ggplot2::geom_density() +   ggplot2::geom_vline(ggplot2::aes(xintercept = -log(p_threshold)), linetype = 2, colour = "purple") +   ggplot2::annotate(     geom = "label", label = paste0("p-val = ", p_threshold),     x = -log(p_threshold), y = 0.15, hjust = 0.25, colour = "purple" ) +   ggplot2::facet_wrap(~n_sp, nrow = 3) +   ggplot2::xlim(0,max(-log(lm_output$lm_table_clean$lm_p))) +   ggplot2::xlab("-log(p-value of linear model)") +   ggplot2::labs(     title = "Distribution of p-values of the linear model",     subtitle = "logarithmic scale"   ) threshold <- 95  lm_output$lm_table_clean |>    tidyr::separate_wider_delim(     cols = unique_curve_id, delim = "_",      names = c("n_sp", "rest"), too_many = "merge") |>   ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = adj_r_squared, colour = n_sp, fill = n_sp)) +    ggplot2::theme_minimal() +   #geom_histogram() +   ggplot2::geom_density(alpha = 0.3) +   ggplot2::geom_vline(ggplot2::aes(xintercept = 0.95), linetype = 2, colour = "purple") +   ggplot2::annotate(     geom = "label", label = paste0("adjusted R2 = ", threshold, "%"),     x = threshold/100, y = 500, hjust = 0.25, colour = "purple" ) +   #facet_wrap(~n_sp, nrow = 3, scales = "free_y") +   ggplot2::xlim(     0.945,     max(lm_output$lm_table_clean$adj_r_squared)) +   ggplot2::xlab("Adjusted R2") +   ggplot2::labs(     title = "Distribution of adjusted R2 values of the linear model"   ) colors <- c("#7FC97F", "#BEAED4", "#FDC086")  lm_output$std_data_clean |>    dplyr::filter_out(dataset == "TDN") |>    ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = as.numeric(std_conc), y = abs_mean, groups = plate_id, colour = dataset, fill = dataset)) +   ggplot2::theme_minimal()+   ggplot2::geom_smooth(     formula = y~x-1, method = "lm", se = TRUE,      alpha = 0.05) +   ggplot2::geom_point() +   ggplot2::facet_wrap(~std_sp, scales = "free") +   ggplot2::scale_color_discrete(palette = colors[1:2]) +   ggplot2::scale_fill_discrete(palette = colors[1:2])

what: 2.2 - QC Standard curves - check conditions of linear model
title: abs-to-conc
text: function suspicious_lm() extracts lm_table` produced plates linear model optimal, .e., either p-value model 0.05, residuals normally distributed, heteroscedasticity residuals. lm_table_suspicious can serve identification outliers visual aid (useful larger data sets, plot_list_lm() creates list plots curve given argument (: suspicious lm’s). Calling individual plots can help spotting possible outlier wells, can removed similar steps shown .  numerous suspicious curves, can take advantage package patchwork` display multiple plots (example hereunder whole lm_table)
code: # extract all plates where "something" is not perfect  (lm_table_suspicious <- lm_table_raw |> suspicious_lm()) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>   dataset plate_id unique_curve_id std_sp  slope r_squared adj_r_squared #>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>           <chr>   <dbl>     <dbl>         <dbl> #> 1 Nmin    NO3_1F1  NO3_1F1_col12   NO3    0.0179     0.999         0.999 #> # ℹ 5 more variables: lm_p <dbl>, normality_lm_residuals <chr>, #> #   shapiro_p <dbl>, homoscedasticity_lm_residuals <chr>, breusch_pagan_p <dbl> suspicious_lm_plotlist <- plot_list_lm(   lm_data = lm_table_suspicious,   std_data = std_corrected)  # check one plot out suspicious_lm_plotlist[[1]] full_plotlist <- plot_list_lm(   lm_data = lm_table_raw,   std_data = std_corrected)   patchwork::wrap_plots(full_plotlist, axis_titles = "collect_y") +      patchwork::plot_annotation(title = "Plots of suspicious Standard curves")

what: 3 - Infer sample concentration from regression equation
title: abs-to-conc
text: Check now left one curve per plate (.e., indeed took per-dilution average) Regression equation Abs = slope * Concentration default vector containing relevant molar masses. Make sure append values relevant study. needed convert concentrations mg molecule per L mg element per L (e.g., mg NO3/L mg N/L) , connect regression data sample absorbance data reg_join_abs(). apply regression equation go absorbance concentration mg N-sp per L convert unit mg N per L using molar_masses convert_molec().

Error t value Pr(>|t|)     #> std_conc       1.672e-02  2.846e-04   58.75 2.70e-08 *** #> I(std_conc^2) -2.127e-05  1.360e-06  -15.64 1.94e-05 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Residual standard error: 0.0229 on 5 degrees of freedom #> Multiple R-squared:  0.9998, Adjusted R-squared:  0.9997  #> F-statistic: 1.363e+04 on 2 and 5 DF,  p-value: 4.551e-10 p_linear <- plot_std(curve, through_origin = TRUE, model = "linear") +    ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "none") +    ggplot2::labs(title = "Linear model") p_poly <- plot_std(curve, through_origin = TRUE, model = "poly") +    ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "none") +    ggplot2::labs(title = "Polynomial model")  p_linear + p_poly # Extract residual data from the 2 models res_linear <- stats::residuals(sum_linear) res_poly <- stats::residuals(sum_poly)  # plot both on the same graph  # plot residuals for linear model plot(curve$std_conc, res_linear, main = "Residuals Analysis", xlab = "Concentration", ylab = "Residuals", col = "grey30", pch = 16) # add red line at y = 0 graphics::abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2) # add residuals for polynomial model graphics::points(curve$std_conc, res_poly,  col = "magenta", pch = 15) # add legend graphics::points(0, y = -0.1, col = "grey30", pch = 16) graphics::text(x = 6, y = -0.1, labels = "linear\nmodel", col = "grey30", adj = 0) graphics::points(0, y = -0.15, col = "magenta", pch = 15) graphics::text(x = 6, y = -0.15, labels = "polynomial\nmodel", col = "magenta", adj = 0) # we take the smallest of the solutions to the 2nd degree equation         (-poly_b + sqrt(poly_b^2 - 4*poly_a*(-abs_corrected))) / (2*poly_a),         (-poly_b - sqrt(poly_b^2 - 4*poly_a*(-abs_corrected))) / (2*poly_a)       )),     .before = std_sp   ) |>    # check it out curve #> # A tibble: 7 × 13 #>   row   column well_id unique_well_id dataset plate_id   unique_curve_id map   #>   <chr> <chr>  <chr>   <chr>          <chr>   <chr>      <chr>           <chr> #> 1 B     1      B1      B1_NO3_TDN_09  TDN     NO3_TDN_09 NO3_TDN_09_col1 Std   #> 2 C     1      C1      C1_NO3_TDN_09  TDN     NO3_TDN_09 NO3_TDN_09_col1 Std   #> 3 D     1      D1      D1_NO3_TDN_09  TDN     NO3_TDN_09 NO3_TDN_09_col1 Std   #> 4 E     1      E1      E1_NO3_TDN_09  TDN     NO3_TDN_09 NO3_TDN_09_col1 Std   #> 5 F     1      F1      F1_NO3_TDN_09  TDN     NO3_TDN_09 NO3_TDN_09_col1 Std   #> 6 G     1      G1      G1_NO3_TDN_09  TDN     NO3_TDN_09 NO3_TDN_09_col1 Std   #> 7 H     1      H1      H1_NO3_TDN_09  TDN     NO3_TDN_09 NO3_TDN_09_col1 Std   #> # ℹ 5 more variables: abs <dbl>, std_sp <chr>, std_unit <chr>, date <date>, #> #   std_conc <dbl> # compute both models lm_linear <- stats::lm(abs ~ 0 + std_conc, data = curve) lm_poly <- stats::lm(abs ~ 0 + std_conc + I(std_conc^2), data = curve) (sum_linear <- summary(lm_linear)) #>  #> Call: #> stats::lm(formula = abs ~ 0 + std_conc, data = curve) #>  #> Residuals: #>      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max  #> -0.18370  0.04129  0.10244  0.13621  0.20977  #>  #> Coefficients: #>           Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)     #> std_conc 0.0124279  0.0004877   25.48 2.41e-07 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Residual standard error: 0.1477 on 6 degrees of freedom #> Multiple R-squared:  0.9908, Adjusted R-squared:  0.9893  #> F-statistic: 649.5 on 1 and 6 DF,  p-value: 2.405e-07 (sum_poly <- summary(lm_poly)) #>  #> Call: #> stats::lm(formula = abs ~ 0 + std_conc + I(std_conc^2), data = curve) #>  #> Residuals: #>         1         2         3         4         5         6         7  #> -0.003064  0.023935  0.025124  0.021264  0.002595 -0.028541  0.011591  #>  #> Coefficients: #>                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)     #> std_conc       1.672e-02  2.846e-04   58.75 2.70e-08 *** #> I(std_conc^2) -2.127e-05  1.360e-06  -15.64 1.94e-05 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Residual standard error: 0.0229 on 5 degrees of freedom #> Multiple R-squared:  0.9998, Adjusted R-squared:  0.9997  #> F-statistic: 1.363e+04 on 2 and 5 DF,  p-value: 4.551e-10 p_linear <- plot_std(curve, through_origin = TRUE, model = "linear") +    ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "none") +    ggplot2::labs(title = "Linear model") p_poly <- plot_std(curve, through_origin = TRUE, model = "poly") +    ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "none") +    ggplot2::labs(title = "Polynomial model")  p_linear + p_poly # Extract residual data from the 2 models res_linear <- stats::residuals(sum_linear) res_poly <- stats::residuals(sum_poly)  # plot both on the same graph  # plot residuals for linear model plot(curve$std_conc, res_linear, main = "Residuals Analysis", xlab = "Concentration", ylab = "Residuals", col = "grey30", pch = 16) # add red line at y = 0 graphics::abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2) # add residuals for polynomial model graphics::points(curve$std_conc, res_poly,  col = "magenta", pch = 15) # add legend graphics::points(0, y = -0.1, col = "grey30", pch = 16) graphics::text(x = 6, y = -0.1, labels = "linear\nmodel", col = "grey30", adj = 0) graphics::points(0, y = -0.15, col = "magenta", pch = 15) graphics::text(x = 6, y = -0.15, labels = "polynomial\nmodel", col = "magenta", adj = 0)

what: 4.2 - Compute polynomial model on Standard curves
title: abs-to-conc
text: steps follow steps section Section 4 , adaptations. First, perform polynomial model NO3 curve individually. background, function lm_std_curve() performs model stats::lm(abs_corrected ~ 0 + std_conc + (std_conc^2), data = curve) individual curve data produces table similar structure one linear model, though columns slightly different reflect differences model. table one row per standard curve. Columns poly_a poly_b correspond coefficients b, respectively, equation y = *x^2 + b*x + c, c = 0 used blank-corrected absorbance model forced go origin y = blank-corrected absorbance x = concentration compound interest (: nitrate)
code: # Compute the polynomial model lm_TDN_raw <- lm_std_curve(   grouped_data = std_corrected_TDN |> dplyr::group_by(unique_curve_id),    model = "poly")

title: abs-to-conc
text: columns lm_TDN_raw contain p-values associated b (poly_a_p poly_b_p, respectively), R2 adjusted R2 values, p-value associated model, results normality test residuals (columns normality_lm_residuals shapiro_p), results test homoscedasticity (columns homoscedasticity_lm_residuals breusch_pagan_p) take subset examine individually. function suspicious_lm() allows subsetting curves linear model doesn’t seem perform ideally. .e., either non-significant model (p-value > 0.05), residuals normally distributed, heteroscedasticity, p-value one coefficients > 0.05. case, concerns 7 curves (32) can visually evaluate plates. visual support, create plot_list_lm() list plots store individual plot “suspicious” standard curves. plot together. Notice “default” curve displayed plots. case, non-normality residuals always issue. p-values (model coefficients) 0.05 residuals heteroscedastic, displayed well.  Whereas shapiro test ran background lm_std_curve() test normality residuals considered trustworthy test, normality easily achieved 8 data points. non-normality result therefore seen aid spot possibly faulty curves, rather strict criterion exclude curves. Visual assessment therefore crucial. case, 7 “suspicious” curves look quite good despite hitting normality threshold. well obviously outside curve, use remove_wells() remove outliers described many times , re-run model, etc. satisfied curves. Per-dilution averages case 2 curves per plate can computed shown linear model. Caution 1: Downward facing parable? functions polynomial model tested case data form downward-facing parable, .e., curve fitted go origin, smallest value x (concentration) correct one solving equation *x^2 + b*x - y = 0 value y (absorbance). upward-facing parable, however, highest solution x right one (provided relationship absorbance concentration ascending curve). data polynomial fit correct, curves look like displayed , check solutions x next sections correct. title: abs-to-conc
text: First, let’s look distribution p-values adjusted R^2 std curve regressions  Now plot curves plot. , check batch-effects assigning date color aesthetics, adapted needed.
code: TDN_p <- density_lm_param(   lm_TDN_raw,    p_or_r = "p", threshold = 0.05,    facetting_std_sp = TRUE, color_std_sp = FALSE)  TDN_adjR2 <- density_lm_param(   lm_TDN_raw, "adjR2", 0.95, color_std_sp = FALSE  )  TDN_p / TDN_adjR2 + patchwork::plot_annotation(title = "Multiple-curve QC of the polynomial model for TDN data") annotation <- std_corrected_TDN |>    dplyr::select(plate_id, abs_corrected, std_conc, date) |>    dplyr::slice_max(std_conc, with_ties = TRUE)   std_corrected_TDN |>    ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(     x = as.numeric(std_conc),      y = abs_corrected, groups = plate_id,     color = date, fill = date)) +   ggplot2::theme_minimal() +    ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "right") +   ggplot2::geom_smooth(     formula = y ~ 0 + x + I(x^2), method = "lm", se = TRUE,      alpha = 0, linetype = 1, linewidth = 0.15) +   ggplot2::geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +   ggplot2::xlab("Standard Concentration [mg N-species / L]") +   ggplot2::ylab("Blank-corrected absorbance") +   ggplot2::labs(title = "Inter-plate variability of the Standard Curves") +   ggplot2::xlim(c(0, 300)) +   ggplot2::annotate(     geom = "text",     x = annotation$std_conc*1.01,      y = annotation$abs_corrected,      label = annotation$plate_id, size = 2,     hjust = 0   )

what: 4.5 - Infer sample concentration from regression equation
title: abs-to-conc
text: use last version linear model data (starts “lm”). case, outlier removal per-dilution average computation necessary, therefore still working lm_TDN_raw. First, join liner-model data (per-plate basis) sample data, .e., row (= sample-containing well) sample data receives additional columns containing polynomial model data. function reg_join_abs() (works linear polynomial model) , can compute concentration N-species mg N / L (.e., mg NO3-N / L, mg NH4-N / L, etc.). concentration standard curve dilution expressed mg N-sp / L (.e., mg NO3 / L mg NH4 /L, etc.), conversion using molar masses target species (N element) origin species (e.g., NO3-) needs take place last step. purposes, plate2N stores molar masses N, NO3-, NO2-, NH4+ vector called molar_masses. script , take smallest value solutions 2nd degree equation *x^2 + b*x - y = 0. See Caution 1 details adapt choice (change min() max()).

title: blank-correction
text: vignette shows basic functions allow correction raw absorbance data (abs) blank-corrected absorbance data, referred abs_corrected throughout package. pipeline adapted case standard curve solutions prepared different blank samples. , 2 parallel pipelines perform blank-correction (first standard curves data, samples data), data standard curves samples merged . theory, “samples” pipeline adaptable case wells used blank (including containing standard curve solutions), though yet tested, bugs may occur. Feel free contact authors suggest improvements. avoid confusion “blank standard curve” “blank samples”, sample-blank referred “extractant” extr throughout vignette, refer solution used extract N-compounds soil samples now serves blank. standard blank referred std_blank.

title: blank-correction
text: fit one row per plate, concentrations standard curve , far, stored compact manner, like : truth, one concentration values corresponds value well standard curve. Note concentration values separated - digits marked ., important function call extract_curve(). Also, concentration values MUST ascending order metadata file. See also ?metadata ?extract_curve() Notice now row contains single value column std_conc many rows? common mistake run extract_curve() raw_meta instead meta. meta one row per plate, whereas raw_meta one row per well, calling extract_curve(raw_meta) result table much long (~96 times long). run issues later, might source. now curve concentrations corresponding dataset, plate_id row plate, can use downstream steps. goes assumption standard curves pipetted vertically complete column 96-well plate. Check ?extract_curve() details. , get subset table raw_meta, keeping absorbance reads wells standard curve (defined value Std column map), remove “old” column std_conc (dilutions curve), replace “new” column std_conc (1 dilution per row). extract_std_blanc() returns list several elements concerning standard blanks std_blank$fetches wells expected contain standard blank, .e., standard data plate-row (pipetting_direction = "top_down") H (pipetting_direction = "bottom_up") std_blank$untrusted identifies expected blank wells correspond lowest absorbance value standard curve1. item may empty std_blank$trusted complement std_blank$untrusted. Check untrusted standard blanks computation per-plate standard curve blank average made trusted blank wells . therefore important check curves containing “untrusted” blank wells decide whether keep function plot_std() allows visualization (subset ) curves.  absorbance well obviously wrong (like ), remove wells computation standard blank average. 2 options : either keeping std_blank$trusted (“untrusted” wells indeed removed) using remove_wells() std_blank$generate better-adapted list “trusted” standard blank wells. Either way, blank averages computed trusted wells (see hereunder). course, works several standard curves problematic plates, otherwise removing std_blank plate2. 2 examples compute average blanks. second option, decide reject one wells std_blank$untrusted: let’s say first one, plate NO3_1F3 (obviously wrong case). Let’s compare options see , indeed, plate NO3_1F5 now received 2 wells compute average (NA values) proposed 2nd option sake example, move first option , decision keep untrusted well plate NO3_1F3 obviously misled. Outlier check whole curve comes later pipeline, search remove outliers prior critical aggregation steps trustworthy otherwise (see also vignette handling-outliers. logic mind, outlier removal wells3 standard curve done later , computing regression absorbance concentration. shown next vignette, abs--conc. confident std_blanks, can use std_blank_avg blank-correct raw absorbance values whole standard curves. done function blank_correct_abs(), also used correct sample absorbance data next section. blank_correct_abs() takes 3 main arguments: raw_wells_data takes standard curves data. ungrouped contain non-blank data (.e., case “top_down” pipetting, rows B H) per_plate_avg_blank takes blank averages (e.g., std_blank_avg) map_to_exclude tells rows exclude raw_wells_data based value column “map”. default setting fits blank-correction sample data (next section), standard data, simply replace "", wish exclude rows can ignore message Joining = join_by(...), just depends additional columns may data tables Notice abs column removed avoid mistaking corrected absorbance. Instead, replaced abs_corrected.

This means that the coefficient of variation is below the threshold of 60%. title: blank-correction
text: string “extr” default argument extr_def extractant_average() can adapted reflect mapping. Like extract_std_blank(..)$average, extractant_average() computes average, standard deviation coefficient variation (%) blanks. 1 extractant per plate? extractant_average() also work several extractants per plate, argument extr_def must given vector extractant (e.g., extr_def = c("extr_1", "extr_2"). See also ?extractant_average() examples.
code: (extr_avg <- extractant_average(raw_meta, extr_def = "extr"))  #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   dataset plate_id map   blank_avg blank_sdev blank_coeff_var_percent #>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>     <dbl>      <dbl>                   <dbl> #> 1 Nmin    NO3_1F1  extr     0.0828   0.000463                   0.559 #> 2 Nmin    NO3_1F2  extr     0.117    0.0657                    56.3   #> 3 Nmin    NO3_1F3  extr     0.0846   0.00151                    1.78  #> 4 Nmin    NO3_1F4  extr     0.0743   0.0268                    36.1   #> 5 Nmin    NO3_1F5  extr     0.0838   0.000463                   0.553

what: 3.3 - Quality check of extractant and outlier removal
title: blank-correction
text: plot_blank_var_distrib() plots distribution coefficient variation throughout data set (becomes relevant big data sets).  big data sets, bound plate one two wells went wrong lab, seeing plates much higher variation can sign need investigate remove outliers (like , maximum coefficient variation almost 60%). can take advantage dplyr::arrange(desc()) sorts rows decreasing values argument, quickly identify suspicious plates (consider removing call desc() negative values coefficients variation). , see suspicious plates NO3_1F2 NO3_1F4. easy small dataset. larger data sets, qc_raw_extr() performs quality check raw absorbance data extractant wells. returns vector containing “suspicious” plate_ids plates exceeding user-defined threshold. Note work raw_meta pipeline, prior outlier removal, course, work “cleanest” data . max_coeff threshold 5% reasonable segregate “acceptable” coefficients , like , 8 wells per plate extractant. get warning message (suppress , opt suppress_warning = TRUE). identifiers suspicious plates recorded suspicious_plate_ids. plates coefficient variation absorbance extractant threshold, qc_raw_extr() returns happy message, instead warning, can also suppressed, using suppress_message = TRUE. obtain full extractant data corresponding suspicious plate_ids, can use suspicious_extr(). Now, remove extractant data (removing 3 rows) Check indeed, 3 rows removed: Finally, re-run average cleaned extractant data Check highest coefficients variation now indeed satisfactory (threshold 5%).
code: # Construct tibble to remove (plate_ids <- suspicious_extr |>      dplyr::ungroup() |>      dplyr::select(dataset, plate_id) |>      unique())  #> # A tibble: 2 × 2 #>   dataset plate_id #>   <chr>   <chr>    #> 1 Nmin    NO3_1F2  #> 2 Nmin    NO3_1F4  # save numbers for plate order in the plot (plate_ids <- plate_ids |>    dplyr::mutate(plate_order = seq(1, nrow(plate_ids)))) #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   dataset plate_id plate_order #>   <chr>   <chr>          <int> #> 1 Nmin    NO3_1F2            1 #> 2 Nmin    NO3_1F4            2 #** !!! MANUAL INPUT !!! *  # Which plate needs 2 outliers removed? plate_with_2_outliers <- c(1)  # Which plate needs no outlier removed? plate_without_outliers <- c(9)   # Which wells are outliers?  well_ids <- c("C8", "D8", "F8") # only fill in well_ids that need to be removed, in the order of the plates to_remove <- plate_ids |>    # double rows for plates with 2 outliers   dplyr::bind_rows(     plate_ids |> dplyr::filter(plate_order %in% plate_with_2_outliers)) |>    #remove plate without outliers   dplyr::filter_out(plate_order %in% plate_without_outliers) |>    #reorder plates (if some were doubled)   dplyr::arrange(plate_order) |>    # add column with well ids from vector defined above   dplyr::mutate(well_id = well_ids) |>    # remove plate_order(optional)   dplyr::select(!plate_order)  # check it out to_remove #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   dataset plate_id well_id #>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>   #> 1 Nmin    NO3_1F2  C8      #> 2 Nmin    NO3_1F2  D8      #> 3 Nmin    NO3_1F4  F8 extr_data_clean <- extr_data |> remove_wells(to_remove) nrow(extr_data) ; nrow(to_remove); nrow(extr_data_clean) #> [1] 40 #> [1] 3 #> [1] 37 extr_avg_clean <- extractant_average(   extractant_data = extr_data_clean, extr_def = "extr") extr_avg_clean |> dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(blank_coeff_var_percent)) |> head() #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   dataset plate_id map   blank_avg blank_sdev blank_coeff_var_percent #>   <chr>   <chr>    <chr>     <dbl>      <dbl>                   <dbl> #> 1 Nmin    NO3_1F3  extr     0.0846   0.00151                    1.78  #> 2 Nmin    NO3_1F2  extr     0.0823   0.000516                   0.627 #> 3 Nmin    NO3_1F4  extr     0.0837   0.000488                   0.583 #> 4 Nmin    NO3_1F1  extr     0.0828   0.000463                   0.559 #> 5 Nmin    NO3_1F5  extr     0.0838   0.000463                   0.553

title: blank-correction
text: Now confident per-plate average value raw absorbance extractant wells, can finally blank-correct sample data. blank_correct_abs() takes 3 arguments: raw_wells_data containing absorbance data needs blank-correcting per_plate_avg_blank contains absorbance data extractant, averaged per plate (one row per plate), based trusted extractant wells map_to_exclude, vector strings representing non-sample data column map within raw_wells_data, .e., blanks, standard curves empty wells. defaults c("empty","Std","extr"). output thus contain blank-corrected sample data. means (linear) modelling standard curve, std_corrected still needed. Let’s look output notice absence value 1 column column (data standard curve4), value extr column map (though present raw_meta still can found extr_id).

for case of 2 extractants: sample_corrected_dbl <-    blank_correct_abs(     raw_wells_data = dbl_extr_plate,      per_plate_avg_blank = extr_avg_clean_dbl,     extr_def = c("extr_1", "extr_2"),     map_to_exclude = c("empty","Std","extr_1", "extr_2"))  #> Joining with `by = join_by(dataset, plate_id, extr_id)` #> Joining with `by = join_by(row, column, well_id, unique_well_id, dataset, #> plate_id, map, extr_id)` # what: 5 - Final note: when absorbance and mapping data with identical plate ids are imported from a single source file.
title: import-tidy
text: Usually, physical plate used lab come 2 data tables plate format. Indeed, measure absorbance well needs accompanied mapping information (e.g., well corresponds also sample id information treatment). Consequently, single plate (let’s call “NO3_plate1”) result import least 2 “plate data tables”. 2 plate data sets characterizing “physical” plate need handled differently downstream analysis. see 2 typical use cases 1) 2 () plate data sets separated separate raw files imported (e.g., absorbance data raw .TXT mapping data recorded .csv). case, 2 distinct tibbles created, quite easy manipulate separately subsequent steps use join_abs_map() merge take profit automatic prefix function adds verticalization/merging process. 2) 2 () plate data sets imported raw file. can done several ways. data imported “skanit csv” mapping plates encoded. default, absorbance mapping data returned 2 separate items list generated skanit_to_csv() can easily manipulated separately, e.g., using join_abs_map() mentioned . data imported single csv file. particular case, recommended name 2 “plate data” distinct plate ids (e.g., “abs-NO3_plate1” “map-NO3_plate1”), simply considered distinct plates function csv_to_skanit(), can easily selected separately subsequent steps, example, taking advantage helper function tidyselect::starts_with() (try verticalized plate: dplyr::select(my_vertical_plate, tidyselect::starts_with("abs"))).

title: prepare_plates
text: Functions introduced vignette mostly helpful preparation step, going lab running absorbance readings 96-well plate reader.

title: prepare_plates
text: map_1_plate() takes single plate_id vector containing sample names returns mapped plate format plate id upper left corner. Usually, one plate needs prepared. Let’s say need prepare 5 plates, list 5 x 14 samples (want exactly 14 samples plate). map_plates() builds map_1_plate() map several plates. interest tibble can easily exported csv, made “pretty” printing usage lab, example readr::write_csv("path///mapping_data.csv) inputted back import pipeline, either csv_to_tibble() tibble post-import steps. Either way, especially tibble exported csv, recommend saving tibble disk, example using readr::wrtite_rds("path///output/data.rds)

text: Transform Raw Absorbance Data Blank-corrected Absorbance

title: Transform Raw Absorbance Data into Blank-corrected Absorbance — blank_correct_abs
code: blank_correct_abs(   raw_wells_data,   per_plate_avg_blank,   extr_def = "extr",   map_to_exclude = c("empty", "Std", "extr") )

title: Transform Raw Absorbance Data into Blank-corrected Absorbance — blank_correct_abs
text: raw_wells_data tibble, based tidy_plates, can contain metadata well. raw_wells_data contains raw absorbance data blank-corrected. Must contain columns "abs" "map". per_plate_avg_blank Contains per-plate average absorbance blank Must contain column "blank_avg" (rename prior function call needed) extr_def string characterizes wells containing extractant mapping (mapcolumn) plate. Defaults "extr". Can vector containing several values (see examples) map_to_exclude vector strings containing map definitions wells data per se (e.g., empty wells, etc.). Defaults c("empty","Std","extr"). wells exclude defined unique "map" (e.g., blank wells standard curve), make sure filter rows raw_wells_data function call.

title: Transform Raw Absorbance Data into Blank-corrected Absorbance — blank_correct_abs
text: tibble blank-corrected absorbance. structure raw_wells_data, abs column removed, column abs_corrected added. output tibble normally contains less rows input tibble (due map_to_exclude)

title: Transform Raw Absorbance Data into Blank-corrected Absorbance — blank_correct_abs

title: Replaces raw absorbance data from standard curves by blank-corrected absorbance values. — correct_std_blank
text: data tibble respecting structure tidy_table. data must , though necessarily order, following column names: row, column, well_id, unique_well_id, dataset, plate_id, map, abs std_def string, defaults "Std": data wells containing standard curve referred . pipetting_direction Can "top_down" (default) "bottom_up". top_down pipetting means curve pipetted vertically (single column 96-well plate), smallest value (blank) row highest value row H. Conversely, bottom_up pipetting blank row H concentrated solution row std_blank_average NULL (default), computed std_blank_trusted. Otherwise, std_blank_average tibble format extract_std_blank(data)$average Changing default value std_blank_average may relevant previous call extract_std_blank() led user correct "trusted" blanks way (see ?extract_std_blank() details) std_blank_trusted NULL (default), extracted std_blank std_blank NULL (default), extracted/computed data, using extract_std_blank().

title: Replaces raw absorbance data from standard curves by blank-corrected absorbance values. — correct_std_blank

title: Import 96-well plate data in csv format — csv_to_tibble
text: Can either comma-delimited ("normal csv" = default setting) semi-colon delimited ("French version" commas used digits semi-colons separators). format must follow strictly following structure, content can numerical (e.g., absorbance data) strings (e.g., plate mapping): plates must directly top (empty rows plates) first plate must top document (empty row ) plates must utmost left document (first 13 columns sheet) data must recorded beyond plate data (nothing plates right) plate name must first cell (top left) plate, just "", marking first row data, just left "1", marking first column data csv files fit neither structure "Skanit" structure (see ?skanit_to_plate details), alternative use read_csv() read_csv2 rearrange resulting tibble fit output given , can inputted downstream steps.

text: cases blank standard curve blank samples, separate blank correction must happen subsets data. , standard curve one well containing blank value, usually pipetted row (pipetting_direction = "top_down"), row H (pipetting_direction = "bottom_up").

title: Extraction and Quality Check (QC) of blank values for standard curves — extract_std_blank
code: extract_std_blank(data, std_def = "Std", pipetting_direction = "top_down")

title: Extraction and Quality Check (QC) of blank values for standard curves — extract_std_blank
text: data tibble respecting structure tidy_table. data must , though necessarily order, following column names: row, column, well_id, unique_well_id, dataset, plate_id, map, abs std_def string, defaults "Std": data wells containing standard curve referred . pipetting_direction Can "top_down" (default) "bottom_up". top_down pipetting means curve pipetted vertically (single column 96-well plate), smallest value (blank) row highest value row H. Conversely, bottom_up pipetting blank row H concentrated solution row

title: Extraction and Quality Check (QC) of blank values for standard curves — extract_std_blank
text: list 4 elements characterizing blank wells: - list$contains supposed blank values (minimum values curve) - list$trusted contains trusted blank values (minimum values wells "correct" row (H)) - list$untrusted contains untrusted wells - list$average contains summarized table per-plate computation average, standard deviation coefficient variation blank values. Note coefficient variation little value computed 2 values, especially values small numbers (typically, blank absorbances often lower 0.1)

title: Extraction and Quality Check (QC) of blank values for standard curves — extract_std_blank
text: extract_std_blank() works steps: - First, data corresponding wells containing standard curve extracted (defined parameter std_def) - Second, within "standard data", dataset, plate column (= 1 curve), smallest absorbance value extracted. - check smallest per-curve value indeed found plate row "" (top_down pipetting) row "H" (bottom_up pipetting). - case, wells considered "untrusted" removed "trusted" blank values. - Per-plate averages blank values computed

title: Merging 2 vertical plates into one — join_abs_map
text: tibble_list list containing tibbles joined (e.g., absorbance tibble, mapping tibble, etc.). Can contain one tibble dataset optional string added prefix column names (tibbles), exception first 2 columns describing well id ("row" "column"). originally meant record name dataset later uses. abs_map string vector add additional prefixes plate names. default value set c("abs", "map"), "abs" data (corresponding first element tibble_list) receive first prefix, "map" data (corresponding second element tibble_list) receive second prefix. Set c("", "") prevent prefix addition. length abs_map must tibble_list. coerce_numeric logical vector decide whether function verticalize_plates(), called separately element tibble_list, coerce data become numeric . default value set FALSE default applied elements. vector length tibble_list can given insted. (e.g., `coerce_numeric = c(FALSE, FALSE)). WARNING: Eventually data pivotted single column, attributing numerics tibbles others may cause issues pivotting step

title: Perform Linear Model for Standard Curve — lm_std_curve
text: grouped_data tibble, grouped per curve (e.g., use dplyr::group_by(plate_id, column) data calling function). Must contain columns std_conc, unique_curve_id abs_corrected model model use. Accepts either linear (default) poly polynomial model.

title: Perform Linear Model for Standard Curve — lm_std_curve
text: table containing relevant parameters linear model, - 1 row per "group" (e.g., plate * column, relevant spot outliers). - columns: unique_curve_id, slope, r_squared, adj_r_squared, lm_p, normality_lm_residuals, shapiro_p, homoscedasticity_lm_residuals, breusch_pagan_p

title: Perform Linear Model for Standard Curve — lm_std_curve
text: Formula within lm(). linear model: y = m*x + c; polynomial model: y = *x^2 + b*x + c; - y = blank-corrected absorbance - x = concentration - c = 0 use blank-corrected absorbance data

text: tibble 1 row per plate, indefinite number columns dataset,plate_id must plate data (dataset manually encoded import-tidy pipeline) std_sp, std_unit, std_conc characterize standard curve. format std_conc, .digit separator -value separator important. Concentration values MUST ascending order

title: Example of plate metadata — metadata
text: Additional recommended columns recording detail extractant. information can easily get lost, especially relevant variations within lab (ex., K2SO4 vs KCl, extractant concentration, ...). , propose record extractant species (K2SO4), extractant unit (M) extractant concentration (0.5). experimental parameters relevant well (e.g., dilution, incubation time, sampling time, researcher, machine used, wavelength,...) Recording date time can help distinguish batch-effects

code: data <- tidy_plates # 0.5 is unreasonable in most uses, but is used here to ensure some output suspicious_extr_per_plate <- qc_raw_extr(data, max_coeff = 5,     suppress_message = TRUE, suppress_warning = TRUE) suspicious_extr <- suspicious_extr(data, max_coeff = 5,     suspicious_extr_per_plate = suspicious_extr_per_plate) #> Joining with `by = join_by(plate_id, map)` multiplot_outlier_extr(suspicious_extractant = suspicious_extr, max_coeff = 5) #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.  ## Tip: if too many curves appear, consider cutting `suspicious_extractant` ##.     into several subsets, to be given as input for multiple runs of `multiplot_outlier_extr()`

title: Compute a list of plots, one per standard curve — plot_list_lm
text: lm_data lm_data shape generated lm_std_curve(), possibly subset observe suspicious curves std_data tibble containing standard data, std_corrected. Absorbance values already blank-corrected model model use. Accepts either linear (default) poly polynomial model.

title: Compute a list of plots, one per standard curve — plot_list_lm
text: list plots, one per curve, annotated issue: - non-significance linear modal (p-value > 0.05) - non-normality residuals (shapiro test threshold p = 0.05) - heteroscedasticity residuals (Breusch-Pagan test, threshold p = 0.05) - model = "poly": non-significance b coefficients regression equation y = ax^2 + bx (p-value > 0.05)

title: Compute a list of plots, one per standard curve — plot_list_lm

code: raw_meta <- tidy_plates |>     dplyr::left_join(metadata, by = dplyr::join_by(dataset,plate_id)) curve_concentration <- extract_curve(metadata) std_data <- raw_meta |>   extract_std_data() |>   dplyr::select(!std_conc) |>   dplyr::left_join(curve_concentration, by = dplyr::join_by(row, dataset, plate_id)) plot_std(std_data, through_origin = FALSE, model = "linear") + ggplot2::facet_wrap(dataset~plate_id)

what: Quality Check (QC) of raw absorbance data — qc_raw_abs
title: Quality Check (QC) of raw absorbance data — qc_raw_abs
text: qc_raw_abs() extracts data relating wells absorbance outside user-defined range. Whereas long-lasting tradition setting "acceptable" absorbance values 0.1 1 (default values function), reality, acceptable range depend experiment, usage quality spectrophotomer. Note values lower 0.1 rare, especially blank lower values standard curve.

title: Quality Check (QC) of raw absorbance data — qc_raw_abs
code: qc_raw_abs(   data,   min_abs = 0.1,   max_abs = 1,   empty_wells = "empty",   show_msg = TRUE,   show_warning = TRUE,   show_plot = TRUE,   plot_binwidth = 0.01,   plot_col_facet = "dataset",   export_plot = NULL )

title: Quality Check (QC) of raw absorbance data — qc_raw_abs
text: data Tibble, similar form tidy_table. particular, columns map, abs, dataset, plate_id, well_id must present named . min_abs, max_abs Lowest highest accepted value absorbance, default 0.1 1, respectively. empty_wells Character string corresponding empty wells described plate mapping. Defaults "empty", can also vector containing several values. Note wells described NA map column also ignored. show_msg, show_warning Whether keep (TRUE) suppress (FALSE) message warning returned case absence (message) presence (warning) suspicious, --range, wells show_plot Whether display associated plot (histogram absorbance values). Default TRUE. Note next arguments relevant show_plot = TRUE. plot_binwidth set binwidth histogram. Default 0.01 plot_col_facet column use facet plots. facetting, choose plot_col_facet = "none" (now, facet 1 axis possible) export_plot Defaults null. Set string save plot global environment, string name object (e.g., export_plot = "abs_distrib" save object called abs_distrib)

title: Quality Check (QC) of raw absorbance data — qc_raw_abs
text: table 5 columns. first 3 (dataset, plate_id, well_id) allow unique identification suspicious wells can used run remove_wells(). next 2 columns (map, abs) allow visualization suspicious wells

title: Quality Check (QC) of raw absorbance data — qc_raw_abs
code: # bring some NA (abs) and empty wells in tidy_table to check that those wells are removed data <- tidy_table data$abs[1] <- NA data$map[2] <- "empty" qc_raw_abs(data) #> Warning: 957 wells out of 958 are out of range for absorbance, i.e., not in the set boundaries of [0.1; 1].  #> See table to identify suspicious wells.   #> # A tibble: 957 × 5 #>    dataset plate_id well_id map     abs    #>    <chr>   <chr>    <chr>   <chr>   <chr>  #>  1 expe1   M17      A1      Std0193 1.5611 #>  2 expe1   M18      A1      Std0289 1.7013 #>  3 expe1   M19      A1      Std0385 1.6865 #>  4 expe1   M20      A1      Std0481 1.7936 #>  5 expe1   M21      A1      Std0577 1.7925 #>  6 expe1   M22      A1      Std0673 1.8274 #>  7 expe1   M23      A1      Std0769 1.9330 #>  8 expe1   M12      A2      Std0002 1.4802 #>  9 expe1   M16      A2      Std0098 1.5590 #> 10 expe1   M17      A2      Std0194 1.5946 #> # ℹ 947 more rows

title: Imports 96-well plate data from .TXT format as exported from the plate reader — txt_to_tibble
text: Imports 96-well plate data .TXT format exported plate reader

title: Imports 96-well plate data from .TXT format as exported from the plate reader — txt_to_tibble
code: txt_to_tibble(filepath, extension = ".TXT", output = "tibble")

title: Imports 96-well plate data from .TXT format as exported from the plate reader — txt_to_tibble
text: filepath path folder containing .TXT files. folder may contain non-TXT files, .TXT files within folder included. extension default ".TXT". parameter defines pattern files included picked folder identified 'filepath'. output Desired output format. Default "tibble." Alternative option list (one element list attributed plate)

title: Imports 96-well plate data from .TXT format as exported from the plate reader — txt_to_tibble
text: Depends output parameter. output = "tibble", tibble plate stacked top (format skanit_to_plate()) output = "list", list element tibble corresponding raw plate data (1 file –> 1 plate –> 1 element). Names elements correspond names files (without extension .TXT). good practice thus name files "plate_name.TXT"

title: Imports 96-well plate data from .TXT format as exported from the plate reader — txt_to_tibble
code: filepath <- system.file("extdata", "txt_examples/", package = "plate2N") abs_tibble <- txt_to_tibble(filepath)

title: From vertical plate data to tidy data using the tidyr package — vertical_to_tidy
code: map_file <- system.file("extdata", "csv_map.csv", package = "plate2N") abs_folder <- system.file("extdata", "txt_examples/", package = "plate2N") map_tibble <- csv_to_tibble(map_file) abs_tibble <- txt_to_tibble(abs_folder) joined_vertical <- join_abs_map(     list(abs_tibble, map_tibble),     dataset = "Nmin-", abs_map = c("abs-", "map-")) (tidy_data <- vertical_to_tidy(joined_vertical, column_def = c("abs", "map"))) #> # A tibble: 480 × 8 #>    row   column well_id unique_well_id dataset plate_id abs   map      #>    <chr> <chr>  <chr>   <chr>          <chr>   <chr>    <chr> <chr>    #>  1 A     1      A1      A1_NO3_1F1     Nmin    NO3_1F1  0.092 Std      #>  2 A     1      A1      A1_NO3_1F2     Nmin    NO3_1F2  0.091 Std      #>  3 A     1      A1      A1_NO3_1F3     Nmin    NO3_1F3  0.110 Std      #>  4 A     1      A1      A1_NO3_1F4     Nmin    NO3_1F4  0.092 Std      #>  5 A     1      A1      A1_NO3_1F5     Nmin    NO3_1F5  0.113 Std      #>  6 A     2      A2      A2_NO3_1F1     Nmin    NO3_1F1  0.114 81_t1_z2 #>  7 A     2      A2      A2_NO3_1F2     Nmin    NO3_1F2  0.107 97_t1_z1 #>  8 A     2      A2      A2_NO3_1F3     Nmin    NO3_1F3  0.095 89_t1_z3 #>  9 A     2      A2      A2_NO3_1F4     Nmin    NO3_1F4  0.118 81_t1_z1 #> 10 A     2      A2      A2_NO3_1F5     Nmin    NO3_1F5  0.167 Std_3_t1 #> # ℹ 470 more rows

title: Tidying plate data (verticalization) — verticalize_plates
text: verticalize_plates brings plate data (absorbance mapping data) vertical, tidy format. starts tibble plate format (rendered txt_to_tibble(), csv_to_tibble() skanit_to_tibble()), ends tidy tibble 96 rows (row per well) one column per plate, well structural columns allowing well identification (row, column)

title: Tidying plate data (verticalization) — verticalize_plates
code: verticalize_plates(tibble, coerce_numeric = FALSE, prefix = NULL)

title: Tidying plate data (verticalization) — verticalize_plates
text: tibble tibble containing raw plate-formatted data tidied. tibble must fit following criteria: - Plate ids single capital letter (e.g., "", "B", ...) - Plates must complete (96 wells accounted , set 12 columns x 8 rows), may contain NA's - Plate data tibble must structured exactly example files (see also txt_to_tibble(), csv_to_tibble() skanit_to_tibble()). coerce_numeric Whether force data entries numerical. default set FALSE, data outputted strings prefix Defaults empty string. prefix can added column names, can useful join tables distinct datasets

title: Tidying plate data (verticalization) — verticalize_plates
text: tidy tibble (verticalized plate data), 1 column per plate

title: Tidying plate data (verticalization) — verticalize_plates
